%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.3 Sketching Signals

> **Section Objective:** Learn how to sketch exponential signals and exponentially varying sinusoids using time constants and envelopes.

---

**Concepts In This Section:**
- decaying exponential
- time constant
- growing exponential
- exponential envelope
- exponentially varying sinusoid

## 1. Exponential sketches use time constants

A decaying exponential is sketched by marking a few reference points, not by plotting dozens of values. For \(e^{-2t}u(t)\), the signal starts at 1 when \(t=0\). The unit step \(u(t)\) makes the signal exactly zero for \(t<0\).

Two checkpoints do all the work:
- After **0.5 s** (one time constant): amplitude drops to \(1/e \approx 0.37\)
- After **1.0 s** (two time constants): amplitude drops to \(1/e^2 \approx 0.135\)

Plot those two points, draw a smooth curve through them starting at 1, and the sketch is done.

### EXAM TIP

When the exponent is \(-at\), read off \(T_c = 1/a\) immediately.

#### Common Trap

Students often write \(T_c = a\). The time constant is \(1/a\), not \(a\).

## 2. The time constant formula

- After one time constant \(T_c\), a decaying exponential \(Ae^{-at}\) has fallen to \(1/e \approx 37\%\) of its starting amplitude \(A\).
- \(A\) is the initial amplitude at \(t=0\); \(a > 0\) is the decay rate; \(T_c = 1/a\) is the time constant.
- Use this formula whenever you see \(Ae^{-at}\) or \(e^{-at}u(t)\) and need to sketch or read off key values.
- **Exam trigger:** any exponential with exponent \(-at\) — find \(T_c\) first.
- **Most common misuse:** writing \(T_c = a\) instead of \(T_c = 1/a\).

$$x(T_c) = A e^{-1}$$

$$T_c = \frac{1}{a}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. B.10](/figures/page-021-fig__b_10-1.png)
*Fig. B.10 shows how decaying exponentials are sketched using time constants and the reference amplitudes \(1/e\) and \(1/e^2\).*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">The figure contains two coordinate plots, both with horizontal axis \(t\) and vertical amplitude axis. Each curve starts at value 1 at \(t=0\) and decreases monotonically toward 0 for \(t&gt;0\). The left plot shows the general case \(e^{-at}u(t)\), marking the reference amplitudes \(1/e \approx 0.37\) and \(1/e^2 \approx 0.135\) at \(t = 1/a\) and \(t = 2/a\) respectively. The right plot shows the specific case \(e^{-2t}u(t)\) with \(a=2\), placing the same amplitude checkpoints at \(t=0.5\) s and \(t=1\) s. Students should notice that after one time constant the exponential falls to about 37% of its initial value, and after two time constants to about 13.5%.</div>

### Worked Example

For \(x(t) = 3e^{-4t}u(t)\):
- Time constant: \(T_c = 1/4 = 0.25\) s
- At \(t=0\): amplitude = 3
- At \(t=0.25\) s: amplitude \(= 3/e \approx 1.10\)
- At \(t=0.50\) s: amplitude \(= 3/e^2 \approx 0.41\)

Sketch starts at 3, passes through those two points, and decays smoothly toward 0.

#### Growing Exponentials

For \(Ae^{at}\) with \(a>0\), the value increases by a factor of \(e\) every \(1/a\) seconds — the same interval logic, but the curve rises instead of falls.

**Quick check:** If \(x(t) = 5e^{-10t}u(t)\), what is the time constant?

**Answer:** \(T_c = 1/10 = 0.1\) seconds.

## 3. Exponentially varying sinusoids are sketched with envelopes

For a signal like \(Ke^{-at}\cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)\), guessing every oscillation point is impractical. The clean method has three steps:

1. Draw the **upper envelope** \(Ke^{-at}\) — a decaying exponential above the axis.
2. Draw the **lower envelope** \(-Ke^{-at}\) — its mirror below the axis.
3. Fit the cosine wave between the two envelopes.

**Textbook example:** \(4e^{-2t}\cos(6t - 60^\circ)\)
- Envelopes start at \(\pm 4\) and decay with \(T_c = 0.5\) s
- Sinusoid period: \(T_0 = 2\pi/6 \approx 1.05\) s
- Phase \(-60^\circ\) shifts the cosine peak to the right

### KEY INSIGHT

The sinusoid **touches** the upper envelope only when \(\cos(\omega_0 t + \theta) = +1\), and touches the lower envelope only when \(\cos(\omega_0 t + \theta) = -1\). Between peaks, the waveform stays strictly inside the envelopes.

### EXAM TIP

- \(a\) controls how fast the amplitude shrinks (envelope decay)
- \(\omega_0\) controls how closely spaced the oscillations are (period)
- \(\theta\) controls the horizontal phase shift of the cosine

$$x(t) = K e^{-at} \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)$$

- This is an **exponentially varying sinusoid**: a cosine whose amplitude shrinks (or grows) over time.
- \(K\) sets the initial envelope size at \(t=0\); \(a > 0\) sets the decay speed; \(\omega_0\) sets the sinusoid frequency; \(\theta\) sets the phase.
- Use this form when a sinusoid's amplitude changes over time — the product of an exponential and a cosine (or sine) is the exam trigger.
- **Envelopes:** upper envelope is \(Ke^{-at}\), lower envelope is \(-Ke^{-at}\).
- **Common misuse:** changing \(a\) and expecting the oscillation period to change (it won't — period depends on \(\omega_0\)); or changing \(\omega_0\) and expecting the envelope shape to change (it won't — envelope depends on \(a\)).

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Time constant \(T_c = 1/a\): after one \(T_c\), a decaying exponential \(Ae^{-at}\) falls to \(x(T_c) = Ae^{-1} \approx 0.37A\).
- Sketch \(Ke^{-at}\cos(\omega_0 t+\theta)\) by drawing envelopes \(\pm Ke^{-at}\) first, then fitting the cosine between them.
- \(a\) controls envelope decay speed; \(\omega_0\) controls oscillation spacing (period \(T_0 = 2\pi/\omega_0\)); \(\theta\) shifts the cosine phase only.
- The sinusoid touches \(+Ke^{-at}\) only when \(\cos = +1\) and touches \(-Ke^{-at}\) only when \(\cos = -1\); elsewhere it stays strictly inside.

*Next, use these sketching habits whenever signals are transformed, combined, or interpreted in later systems problems.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
